t: COWS (Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale) — withdrawal severity.
(in a maintenance clinic, under daily supervision) — full μ-agonist; 60–120 mg/day; long half-life; QTc monitoring.
— partial μ-agonist + naloxone; office-based prescription; low overdose potential; "induction" — started in mild withdrawal condition.
OAT significantly reduces mortality and HIV transmission (MacArthur G.J. et al. BMJ 2012 meta-analysis).

OAT is superior to psychosocial intervention in mortality reduction; however, combination with psychosocial intervention (MI, CBT, contingency management, peer support, 12-step — see "Treatment methodologies") is optimal.
(oral 50 mg or IM Vivitrol 380 mg monthly) — patient must be abstinent for 7–10 days before initiation; in motivated patients or in the absence of formal maintenance programs.
Methadone or buprenorphine — OAT is continued during pregnancy; detox is contraindicated (risk of relapse and pregnancy loss).
NAS intervention in the newborn — morphine or methadone.
WHO — OAT is the gold standard, superior to abstinence-oriented approach.
— Office-based; "Suboxone induction" protocol; weksloid has lower overdose potential.
Long-term stabilization and mortality reduction with OAT.
Myth 1: "OAT (methadone, buprenorphine) replaces one addiction with another"
OAT is medical treatment — significantly reduces mortality, HIV, crime, social disruption; "sensitive" anti-OAT stance is not evidence-based. WHO, SAMHSA, NICE — OAT is the gold standard.
Myth 2: "Detoxification is sufficient, OAT is not needed"
detox alone 90%+ relapse; overdose mortality highest immediately after detox (loss of tolerance). OAT reduces mortality by 50%+.
UROD associated with deaths and serious adverse effects; no evidence of efficacy; SAMHSA and NICE do not recommend.
Myth 4: "Detox is needed in a pregnant woman"
detox in pregnancy is contraindicated; OAT (methadone or buprenorphine) is maintained.
Myth 5: "Ibogaine or ayahuasca cures opioid addiction"
Not evidenced; ibogaine accompanied by cardiac arrhythmia and fatal cases (FDA warning); not in standard practice.
SAMHSA TIP 63 — methadone and buprenorphine are more effective in reducing mortality; naltrexone in motivated abstinent patients.
Myth 7: Naloxone take-home programs encourage use
Walley A.Y. et al. BMJ 2013 — community naloxone programs significantly reduce overdose deaths; do not increase use.
WHO. ICD-11. 6C41 Disorders due to use of opioids. 2024.
SAMHSA. TIP 63: Medications for Opioid Use Disorder. 2021.
MacArthur G.J. et al. Opioid substitution treatment and HIV transmission in people who inject drugs. BMJ 2012;345:e5945.
Walley A.Y. et al. Opioid overdose rates and implementation of overdose education and nasal naloxone distribution. BMJ 2013;346:f174.
